summary: Gruber argues that Apple TV is the product where Apple's premium pricing strategy breaks down. While Apple successfully charges more for MacBooks, iPads, and iPhones because the quality difference is obvious, the Apple TV 4K at $179 faces a much harder sell against Amazon's $70 Fire TV and cheap Roku devices that handle the same core task of 4K streaming. He notes that even loyal Apple ecosystem users are balking at the price, and that Apple TV's advantages — computing power, gaming potential, iTunes lock-in — aren't compelling enough for people who primarily stream from Netflix and other services. Gruber concludes that Apple is ceding market share by refusing to compete on price in a category where it can't make a convincingly superior product.
key_points: The new Amazon Fire TV matches Apple TV 4K's headline feature (4K HDR playback) at less than half the price ($70 vs $179)
Apple's premium pricing works for PCs, tablets, and phones but fails for set-top boxes because the quality gap isn't obvious enough to justify the cost
iTunes is Apple TV's main differentiator, but most consumers now stream rather than buy movies, undermining that advantage
Even loyal Apple ecosystem users — MacBook, iPad, iPhone owners — are refusing to pay double for Apple TV
Apple's older non-4K Apple TV at $149 is arguably worse at its primary job than devices costing half as much
money_quotes: I'm hearing from a lot of people who are in the Apple ecosystem — people who own MacBooks, iPads, and iPhones — who just don't want to spend $200 for an Apple TV when they can get a Roku or Fire TV for a lot less.
I think there are a lot of people who look at iPhones, iPads, and MacBooks and see them as 'expensive but worth it' but who look at Apple TV and see it as 'ridiculously overpriced'.
It's not enough to make a better set-top box. It has to be obviously better. I don't think Apple TV's current lineup makes that case.
The fact that it's a more powerful computer is irrelevant. And yet it costs twice as much.
The primary selling point of an Apple TV over these devices is iTunes.
Apple TV is the product line where I think that might really be a problem.
key_insight: Apple's premium pricing strategy fails for Apple TV because in the set-top box market, the quality difference isn't obvious enough to justify costing more than twice the competition.
